content.full_text:
California
Fair P01itical
Practices Cornmission
Karen Bass′  Accountant
char■es E. Bray′  cPA
2626 Nich01son Street
San Leandro′  CA  94577
August 20′  ■990
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. A-9o-474
Dear Ms. Bass:
You have requested advice on behalf of sheriff Charles
Plummer concerning his duties under the ne$, rrpersonal use'l
provisions of the Political Reforn Act (the rristrr1.1/
OUESTTON
May the sheriff use carnpaign funds to pay for a trip to China
under the auspices of the citizen Ambassador Program?
CONCLUSION
Since the trip to China is related to the sheriff's elective
office, it has a governmental, purpose. Therefore, the use of
carnpaign funds for payments or rej.mbursements for the ordinary and
necessary expenses associated with the trip, including travel and
accornmodations, is pernissible. 
Ilowever, the Commission
interprets the applicable Internal Revenue Code Sections 162 and
274 to aLlow the use of canpaign funds only for the business
portions of the trip.
FACTS
Sheriff charles c. Plunmer of Alameda county has been invited
to visit China as a mernber of a smalL delegation (30) of North
American professional and citizens leaders involved in the
l/ 
Governnent Code Sections 81OOO-91015. Al-I statutory
references are to the covernment Code unless otherwise indicated.
Conrnission regulations appear at 2 CaLifornia Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the Californj.a Code of Regulations.
428 J Strcet,Suite 800 ● P.0.Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804´ 0807 ● (916)322‐ 5660


Our File No. A-90-474
Page 2
adninistration of juvenile and criminal justice. 
The project is
being organized under the auspices of the Citizen Ambassador
Program. The citizen Ambassador Program will coordinate travel
and professional arrangements with the program's hosts, the
chinese Bar Association.
The delegation will convene in Beijing on october 9, will
visit Beijing, Shanghai, Hangzhou, and Hong Kong, and return to
San Francisco on october 23, 1990 (15 days). The estimated
expense per delegate of 93,175 includes all professional
activities, 
transportation within china, accomnodations, all neals
in China, uost meals in Hong Kong, and cultural and sightseeing
activities. 
Delegates are responsible for their own air
connections to Beijing and from Hong Konq.
ANALYSI S
Effective January 1, 1990, the Act was aroended by Senate
Bill 1431 to include new provisions that regrulate the apPropriate
use of campaign funds. (Section 85800, et seq.) The purPose of
the J.egislation was rrto ensure that candidates, elected officers
and the people clearfy recognize acceptable and unacceptable uses
of campaign funds.'r (senate Elections Comrnittee Analysis of
sB 1431. )
The general rule of the new personal use 1aw is that any
expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a niniruun, reasonably
related to a political, 
legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 85801.) Horrever, where an expenditure confers a
substantial personaL benefit on the candidate or officeholder, the
expenditure must be directly related to a political, 
legislative,
or governmental purpose. (Section 85801.) A substantial personal
benefit is defined as a dj-rect personaf benefit to the candidate
or officeholder of $100 or more. (Section 85800(b) (2).)
lIoreover, the new personal use Iaw sets forth provisions
dealing with specific types of expenditures. Section 85802(a) (1),
which deafs with travel, provides:
(a) Canpaign funds shalL not be used to pay
or reinburse the candidate, the elected officer, or
eurployees or staff of the campaign commi,ttee or the
elected officer's governmental agency for travel
expenses and necessary accornmodations except lrhen
these expenditures are directlv related to a
political, 
leqislative. or qovernmental purpose,
(1) For purposes of this section, paynents or
reinbursements for travel and necessary acconmoda-
tions shal1 be considered as directly related to a
po),itical, Iegislative, or governmental purpose if


Our File No. A-90-474
Page 3
the paynents would meet standards similar to the
standards of the Internal Revenue Service pursuant
to Sections 162 and 274 of the fnternal Revenue
code for deductions of travel expenses under the
federal incorne tax Iaw.
(Hnphasis added. )
According to Section 85802(a)(1), the use of canpaj,gn funds
for payments or reimbursenents for travel and necessary acconmoda-
tions is pernissible if the payments meet standards similar to the
standards for the deduction of travel expenses pursuant to
Internal Revenue code Sections !62 and 274. The commission
presently is considering regutations interpreting this section'
ihus, it is possible that the Comroission rnay adopt regulations
which uray be at variance with the advice given in this letter.
However, until the Commission adopts reguLations, we are
interpreting Section 85802(a) (1) to reguire a political,
legistative or governmental purpose for the travel and
accomrnodations that are clearly associated with either the
elective office held or the el.ective office sought by the
officj.al.2/ 
rf there is a political, 
leqislative or governmental
purpose, carnpaign funds uay be used for ordinary and necessary
expLnses associated lrith the travel and accomltodation consistent
wiln tne Internal Revenue Service standards.3/
fnternal Revenue Code Section 162(a) (2) sets forth general
rules which pernit the deduction of all ordinary and necessary
travelinq expenses while away from home in the pursuit of a trade
or business. In light of the purpose behind the new personal use
Iaw, we have interpreted the Internal Revenue Code Sections to
a1low only for the use of carnpaign funds for the "business
portions" of the trip. 
Thus, the 15-day trip to china nay be
broken into business days (days on which there are rneetings and
field visits) and nonbusiness days. where a weekend falls between
business days, the weekend wilL also be counted as business tirne.
By our calculations twelve-fifteenths of the fee for the trip nay
be paid fron the campaign funds of elected officials.
2/ Section 85202 provides that carnpaign funds are held in trust
for expenses associated with the office held or sought. Here,
such an association rrould appear to be present if the purpose of
the trip is to acquire infonnation regarding alternative
enforcenent techniques which rnay be adaptable for use in the
sheriff 's jurisdiction,
3/ The connission has not yet defined a rrgovernmental purposerr
under the personal use sections. However, it appears fron the
attached letter and proposed schedule of activities that the trip
is directly related to a governmental purpose.


However, no part of expenditures for solely personal
purposes, such as some of the rneals, entertainment, guided
cultural and sight-seeing activities, 
may be paid for with
canpaign funds. consequently, since ttrese Personal expenses
appear to be part of the rrpackage price,rr they rnust be subtracted
out of the total fee prior to the proration of the fee.
If you have any further questions regarding this natter,
please feel free to contact ne at (916) 322-5901.
Our Fi■e No. A-90-474
Page 4

Sincerely,
scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel

CONCLUSION: Since the trip to China is related to the sheriff's elective

Ilowever, the Commission
interprets the applicable Internal Revenue Code Sections 162 and
